does: This paper proposes JailExpert, an automated framework for generating jailbreak prompts that attack safety measures in large language models. The core contribution is introducing a formal representation of attack experience that captures past jailbreak attempts, clustering these experiences based on semantic similarity, and enabling dynamic updates to an experience pool as new attacks succeed or fail. The framework leverages accumulated attack experiences to inform new jailbreak attempts, rather than optimizing each attack independently from scratch. Experiments demonstrate that JailExpert improves efficiency and effectiveness of jailbreak generation compared to existing iterative mutation and dynamic optimization approaches that lack memory of previous attack patterns.
connects: This work builds on a growing line of research into jailbreaking techniques and LLM vulnerabilities, extending prior work that uses iterative mutation and automated prompt optimization to find adversarial inputs. It responds directly to limitations in methods like those based on genetic algorithms and gradient-based optimization for generating attack prompts, which are inefficient because they treat each attack attempt in isolation. The paper connects to broader adversarial robustness literature in machine learning and safety research focused on identifying model vulnerabilities, while also engaging with knowledge distillation and experience replay concepts from reinforcement learning that inform how past interactions can guide future optimization.
enables: This framework opens avenues for more efficient adversarial testing and red-teaming of LLMs by establishing formal methods to accumulate and reuse attack knowledge. The work enables researchers to build comprehensive vulnerability databases structured by attack semantic properties, supporting faster iteration cycles in identifying model weaknesses. Future directions include scaling JailExpert across multiple models to understand transferability of attack experiences, integrating findings into robustness training methods, and exploring how similar experience-based optimization could improve other aspects of automated prompt engineering. The structured representation of attack experiences could also inform the development of more targeted defense mechanisms by revealing common patterns in successful jailbreaks.
